title: Target specific peptide design using latent space approximate trajectory collector
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"gub4w","text":"Despite the prevalence and many successes of deep learning applications in de novo molecular design, the problem of peptide generation targeting specific proteins remains unsolved. A main barrier for this is the scarcity of the high-quality training data. To tackle the issue, we propose a novel machine learning based peptide design architecture, called Latent Space Approximate Trajectory Collector (LSATC). It consists of a series of samplers on an optimization trajectory on a highly non-convex energy landscape that approximates the distributions of peptides with desired properties in a latent space. The process involves little human intervention and can be implemented in an end-to-end manner. We demonstrate the model by the design of peptide extensions targeting β-catenin, a key nuclear effector protein involved in canonical Wnt signalling. When compared with a random sampler, LSATC can sample peptides with 36% lower mean binding scores in a 16 times smaller interquartile range (IQR) and 284% less mean hydrophobicity with a 1.4 times smaller IQR. LSATC also largely outperforms other common generative models.Finally, we utilize a clustering algorithm to select 4 peptides from the100  LSATC  designed peptides for experimental validation. The resultconfirms that all the four peptides extended by LSATC show improved β-catenin binding by at least 20.0%, and two of the peptides show a 3 fold increase in binding  affinity as compared to the base peptide.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"t2zlx","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"kvmcf","text":"Therapeutic  peptides  are  a  class  of  pharmaceutical  agents  that  are  distinctfrom  small  molecule  drugs  due  to  their  unique  biochemical  and  therapeuticcharacteristics. In recent years, many peptide drugs have been found to havesuperior potency and safety profiles than small molecule drugs [1]. Peptidescan disrupt unwanted protein-protein interactions (PPI) that have often beenimplicated  to  play  a  role  in  cancer  development  and  progression.  One  suchexample is the deregulation of Wnt/beta-catenin/T-cell factor (Tcf) signalingcommon  in  many  human  cancers.  Thus,  the  development  of  peptide-basedPPI inhibitors has become one of the most topical directions in cancer drugresearch.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"sa1bb","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"43f2a","text":"Structure-based  design  of  peptide  inhibitors  for  a  specific  protein  tar-get  has  long  been  an  empirical  task.  Traditionally,  peptide  design  has  beenfocused  on  sequence  perturbation,  including  residue  mutation,  interchainresidue exchange, alanine scanning and chemical modification [2, 3], which isguided by structural information obtained for the protein or protein-peptidecomplex  system.  The  key  limitation  of  this  strategy  is  the  negligence  ofpotential  secondary  structure  changes  upon  sequence  perturbation  and  howthe resulting changes may shift the protein-peptide binding structures. High-Throughput-Screening (HTS) has also been widely applied in peptide design.HTS is a brute force method to identify bioactive peptides by rapidly conduct-ing thousands to millions of biochemical, genetic, or pharmacological assays.However, HTS demands highly specialized instrumentation, development andadoption  of  appropriate  bioassays,  and  high  quality  peptide  libraries.  Addi-tionally, actives discovered in HTS are often serendipitous [4]. To complementHTS, virtual screening, such as peptide docking, has been heavily used for pep-tide inhibitor design [5]. Peptide docking is usually composed of a samplingtechnique to explore peptide conformations and a scoring function to evalu-ate the strength of peptide binding for all sampled peptide binding poses. Thetwo-step protocol takes at least minutes to evaluate one protein-peptide com-plex [6], which limits its ability in sequence exploration and thus its use in denovo peptide design.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"flykg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"u4yfx","text":"Springer Nature 2021 LATEX templateArticle Title3In  recent  years,  machine  learning-based  molecular  design  has  witnessedrapid  development.  A  very  important  area  in  deep  generative  models  is  theefficient representation of molecules. Prior to the deep learning era, fingerprintdescriptors such as Morgan fingerprints [7] for small molecules and atom-pairfingerprints for proteins [8] were prevalent. However, these representations thatencode the chemical and structural information of individual molecules are nottask  specific  [9].  Deep  learning  models  have  been  developed  to  address  thislimitation by learning a unified representation in a large dataset and then finetuning this representation for a specific task. During the past few years, theresearch  on  molecular  representation  has  been  shifted  to  string  transforma-tion, most of which has borrowed the idea from natural language processing.There are two major types of model framework. The first is the recurrent neu-ral network, such as Long-Short Term Memory (LSTM) [10], Gated RecurrentUnits [11] and Recurrent Attention [12]. These models have been utilized topredict  molecular  properties,  such  as  solubility,  toxicity  [13–15].  The  othertype is transformer [16] based models, which incorporate a multi-head atten-tion mechanism to process sequential data more efficiently. The transformerbased architectures such as ProteinBert [17] and ProteinTrans [18] have beenfrequently  used  in  protein  representation,  and  have  shown  great  success  inmultiple protein downstream classification or regression tasks (e.g. secondarystructure classification, fluorescence prediction). Recently, the graph represen-tation  of  molecules  has  gained  great  attention  due  to  the  graph’s  ability  toinclude  more  detailed  structural  and  spatial  information  [19].  Graph  neuralnetwork has been applied to process molecular graphs and perform propertyprediction [20–22].","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"j0g2o","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"rsx3x","text":"Although the learned molecular representation has been exploited in manyproperty  prediction  models,  few  models  are  about  molecular  generation.  In2017, Bjerrum used the RNN network to generate chemically valid molecules[23].  Since  then,  several  studies  have  been  published  on  the  generation  ofmolecules with optimized general properties such as logP, TPSA and QED [24–26], while work on de novo protein or peptide design has been scarce, and mostof it has focused on the design of a single protein with certain desired prop-erties, such as length, stability and isoelectric point [27, 28]. Design of smallmolecule or peptide therapeutics targeting a specific protein has been even lessexplored. In 2020, Das et al. proposed a method for antimicrobial drug designby using rejection sampling to search for appropriate molecular SMILES in alatent space [29]. In 2022, Castro et al. proposed a gradient based latent spacesearch method to design protein sequences against the ranibizumab antibody[30].  The  two  papers  are  most  relevant  to  our  work,  albeit  the  first  work  isnot on peptide design and the second limits its application to a special caseprotein  that  has  an  existing  dataset  of  60,000  samples.  The  main  challengein protein-specific peptide design is the lack of accurate binding affinity datacoupled  with  the  immense  peptide  sequence  space.  To  our  knowledge,  deeplearning models for protein specific peptide generation don’t yet exist.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"gt8wc","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"vd62f","text":"In this paper, we propose a novel protein specific peptide generation scheme(Figure 1), called Latent Space Approximate Trajectory Collector (LSATC).We implement a GRU based Wasserstein auto encoder (WAE) [31] to obtaina 1D continuous latent space representation of peptides, as shown in Figure1(a).  To  circumvent  the  data  scarcity  problem,  we  design  a  feedback  loopusing CMA-ES [32] to optimize the generator in a peptide latent space andthen collect samples on the generator’s trajectory. The process is illustrated inFigure 1(c). A fast feedback evaluator is crucial to the CMA-ES optimization.Since most structure-based binding evaluation (e.g., computational docking)would be computationally too costly, we train an efficient surrogate model toboost  the  evaluation  time  from  minutes  to  microseconds  (Figure  1(b)).  Forthe surrogate model training, the binding energies and the hydrophobicitiesof  a  reasonably  small  number  of  randomly  sampled  peptides  are  evaluatedusing Pyrosetta and Biopython, respectively. Finally, we sample peptides onthe  explored  trajectory  whose  associated  losses  are  lower  than  a  predefinedthreshold value. We note that the increase in speed offered by the surrogatemodel enables us to fully explore the peptide encoder space for peptide designand optimization. Additionally, the incorporation of the biophysics-based sur-rogate model into our deep learning scheme also improves the interpretabilityof our LSATC model and facilitates the final peptide candidate ranking andselection.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"esqrd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"hxbpd","text":"We  test  our  LSATC  model  in  a  multi-objective  peptide  extension  task,which aims to improve the binding of a base peptide“YPEDILDKHLQRVIL”with beta-catenin by extending its N-terminus, while maintaining or loweringthe hydrophobicity of the extended peptides to minimize non-specific binding.For  simplicity,  we  only  consider  peptide  extensions  of  5  amino  acids,  whichlimits the search space to 3.2 million peptides. However, it is worth noting thatLSATC is not limited to the generation of fixed length peptides. The entire pep-tide extension process, including the dataset generation and model training,takes two days to finish on a local 16-core CPU computer. Our LSATC modelis proven to be much more efficient in generating desired peptides than randomgeneration and several other commonly used generative models. The experi-mental results show that our generated peptides are both more potent and lesshydrophobic than the base peptide, with the highest binding improvement by3 fold.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"om93s","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"hpkdh","text":"Our contributions are:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"au2v4","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"vgxkd","text":"\u003ccode\u003e•\u003c/code\u003eWe  build  a  de  novo  peptide  generator  that  can  efficiently  generate  morepotent peptide sequences targeting a specific protein when no or very littlebinding data exist.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ulbok","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"rnxop","text":"\u003ccode\u003e•\u003c/code\u003eWe  design  a  series  of  samplers  on  an  optimization  trajectory  in  a  latentspace,  which  not  only  solves  the  data  scarcity  problem  in  target  specificpeptide design, but also improves the interpretability of the model.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fe4kh","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"my1gj","text":"\u003ccode\u003e•\u003c/code\u003eOur  proposed  design  pipeline  is  highly  automated  and  experimental  testready.  We  combine  a  physics-based  scoring  scheme  with  machine  learn-ing  models  to  systematically  prioritize  a  subset  of  promising  peptides  forexperimental testing from a pool of LSATC generated peptides.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"q1sej","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6fnf9","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: labeled dataset: cd 'data_generation/binding_energy_test' python get gt dataset.py put the generated data into 'LSATC/data/dataset' folder
unlabeled dataset: cd 'LSATC/data' python generate unlabeled data.py put the generated data into 'LSATC/data/dataset' folder
latent space search cd 'LSATC/seq_sampling' python CMA ES prop.py
CWAE cd 'LSATC/comparing_CVAE' python create_dataset.py to create categorical label put the categorical label dataset into 'LSATC/data/dataset' folder python train_CVAE.py
GMM cd 'LSTAC/comparing VAE GMM' python train_model.py
cd 'LSATC/seq_sampling' you should be able to find all the analysis figures by running figure 2.py, figure 3.py, figure 4.py and support information.py

1. WAE is properly trained so that the reconstruction loss and the miss match is close to 0 2. Property prediction network is properly trained so that on the test dataset the hydrophobicty prediction is almost perfect and the mean prediction of the binding enery is around 0.8. 3. CMA-ES based trajectory collector can sample peptides whose mean performance (hydrophobicty rank + binding energy rank) is much lower than that of the labelled dataset and the IQR is much narrower than that of the labelled dataset. 4. You shall be able to run the "results evaluations" in Procedure to get all the computational evaluation results similar to those presented in the paper. 5. We anticipate that if you perform the Gibbs Cluster on the sampled peptides from LSATC for the experiments, you will get much lower binding evaluation than that of your base peptide.

Figure 1. (a). Peptide extension reconstruction network. A reconstruction loss is used. Thedistribution of the encoding is regularized to a Gaussian distribution. (b). Property predic-tion network. The model estimates the hydrophobicity and the binding energy of a peptideextension. (c). Optimization process for a generative model. A Gaussian sampler in the latentspace is learned using CMA-ES. A reconstruction network is used in decoding. A propertyprediction network is used as a surrogate model of Pyrosetta for the evaluation of generatedpeptides. The samplers are recorded in every iterations. The total loss includes the desiredpeptide properties and the penalty of sampling invalid encodings Figure 2. (a), The regression plot of the log of in vitro IC50 and binding energies calculatedby &nbsp;MM/GBSA. (b), The regression plot of the log of in vitro IC50 and binding energies calculated by Rosetta FlexPepDock. (c), The regression plot of the log of in vitro IC50 and binding energies calculated by flex ddG. (d), The regression plot of the log of in vitro IC50and binding energies calculated by flex ddG(gam). (e), The regression plot of the log of invitro IC50 and binding energies calculated by Pyrosetta. Figure 3. (a), The direct reconstruction loss (left) and the sampling reconstruction loss (right)during the training. (b), An illustration of the difference between the direct reconstructionand the sampling reconstruction. Their reconstruction results on VAE and WAE are shown.(c), &nbsp;An &nbsp;example &nbsp;optimization &nbsp;trajectory &nbsp;for &nbsp;a &nbsp;decoder &nbsp;with &nbsp;low &nbsp;sampling &nbsp;reconstructionaccuracy. The sampler is an optimizer in a latent space different from the one described bythe encoder (denoted as the dataset). Figure 4. The correlation plot of the predicted vs. ground truth binding scores. The<code>r2</code>valueis calculated between the prediction mean in a small interval and the mean of the true valueswhose corresponding predictions fall into the interval. The uncertainty represents the rangeof the ground truth at each predicted value. Figure 5. (a), The CMA-ES training loss. There are high loss regions caused by the invalidpeptide penalty and low loss regions enriched with valid peptides. In the low loss regions,the primary goal is to reduce the property losses. (b), The average binding scores duringthe optimization (left). The average hydrophobicity during the optimization (right). (c), Anillustration &nbsp;of &nbsp;the &nbsp;sampler’s &nbsp;behavior &nbsp;during &nbsp;the &nbsp;optimization &nbsp;(d), &nbsp;The &nbsp;magnitude &nbsp;of &nbsp;thecovariance during the optimization. The magnitude is calculated as the sum of the absolutevalues of all the elements (left). A 2d-tnse plot to show the trajectory of the sampler mean.The sampler searches around the periphery of the labelled dataset (right). (e), A 2d-pca plotto show the preferential direction of the encoding change. It is obvious that most searchesoccur in the bottom left corner on the edge of the labelled dataset (left). The value changesfor 5 encoding dimensions during the optimization. They are tuned to either a positive or anegative direction, indicating the tuner has a directional preference (right). Figure 6. (a), 2d-tsne plot to show how the samples from the different generative models aredistributed during the training of high quality peptides. The models are LSATC (left), GMM(middle) and CWAE (right). (b), Quality comparisons of the sampled peptides from randomselection, LSATC, CWAE and GMM. (c), A logo plot to show the most sampled amino acidsat each extension position. (d), The depletion of valid encodings. The sampling batch is fixedfor each iteration. It is obvious that the required iterations rapidly increase as the numberof the requested peptides increases except for the k-means selection. (e), The optimizationtrajectory for the sampler and the final selected samplers using different selection strategies. Figure 7. The logo plots of the LO matrices for the four peptide clusters. The x-axis is theamino acid position, which has a total length of 5. The y-axis is the information gain of anamino acid at that position compared with its background frequency. (a),(b),(c),(d) are thelogo plots of the LO matrices for cluster 1 to 4, respectively. In a logo plot, the top aminoacid in each column represents the most likely amino acid at that position.
